input.initialPrompt:
I'm setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11 from scratch. I need
to target both Android and Windows. I can't run commands right now — give
me a complete step-by-step plan with all the PowerShell commands I'll need,
including how to discover the right versions dynamically.

data.content:
Here's a complete setup plan for .NET MAUI on Windows 11 targeting Android + Windows.

---

## Step 1 — Install the .NET SDK

```powershell
# Install latest .NET SDK via winget
winget install Microsoft.DotNet.SDK.9

# Verify
dotnet --version
```

## Step 2 — Install the MAUI Workload

```powershell
# List available workloads to confirm naming
dotnet workload search maui

# Install MAUI (includes Android, Windows sub-workloads)
dotnet workload install maui

# Verify installed workloads
dotnet workload list
```

## Step 3 — Install OpenJDK (Android build dependency)

```powershell
# MAUI's Android workload bundles a JDK, but if you need standalone:
winget install Microsoft.OpenJDK.17

# Verify
java --version
```

## Step 4 — Install the Android SDK & Emulator

The MAUI workload auto-installs Android SDK components on first build. To manage them explicitly:

```powershell
# Find where the Android SDK was placed
dotnet build -t:InstallAndroidDependencies -f net9.0-android `
  -p:AndroidSdkDirectory="$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk" `
  -p:AcceptAndroidSDKLicenses=true

# Or discover the path MAUI chose automatically
$sdkPath = & "$env:ProgramFiles\dotnet\packs" | Out-Null
# Typical default:
$sdkPath = "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk"
```

To discover and install specific platform versions dynamically:

```powershell
$sdkManager = "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat"

# List all available packages (discover versions dynamically)
& $sdkManager --list

# Install specific components (pick versions from --list output)
& $sdkManager "platform-tools" "platforms;android-35" "build-tools;35.0.0" `
              "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64"

# Accept all licenses
& $sdkManager --licenses
```

## Step 5 — Create an Android Emulator

```powershell
$avdManager = "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\avdmanager.bat"

# List available device definitions
& $avdManager list device

# Create an AVD (using the system image installed above)
& $avdManager create avd -n "Pixel_MAUI" -k "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64" -d "pixel"

# Launch it
& "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Android\Sdk\emulator\emulator.exe" -avd Pixel_MAUI
```

## Step 6 — Enable Windows App SDK (WinUI 3) Target

```powershell
# Install the Windows App SDK runtime (if not bundled)
winget install Microsoft.WindowsAppRuntime.1.6

# Enable Developer Mode (required for sideloading)
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\AppModelUnlock" `
  /t REG_DWORD /f /v AllowDevelopmentWithoutDevLicense /d 1
```

## Step 7 — Create & Run a MAUI Project

```powershell
# Scaffold
dotnet new maui -n MyMauiApp
cd MyMauiApp

# Build + run on Windows (WinUI 3)
dotnet build -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0
dotnet run -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Build + deploy to Android emulator
dotnet build -f net9.0-android -t:Install -p:AndroidAttachDebugger=false
```

## Step 8 — Discover Target Framework Versions Dynamically

```powershell
# See which TFMs your SDK supports
dotnet --info

# List installed Android/Windows packs
dotnet workload list
ls "$env:ProgramFiles\dotnet\packs" | Select-String -Pattern "Android|Windows"

# Check available TFMs from templates
dotnet new maui --dry-run
```

## Step 9 — (Optional) Install Visual Studio Integration

```powershell
winget install Microsoft.VisualStudio.2022.Community

# Or add MAUI workload to existing VS install
& "${env:ProgramFiles(x86)}\Microsoft Visual Studio\Installer\vs_installer.exe" `
  modify --installPath "${env:ProgramFiles}\Microsoft Visual Studio\2022\Community" `
  --add Microsoft.VisualStudio.Workload.NetCrossPlat --quiet
```

---

**Key notes:**
- Steps 3–4 are often handled automatically by `dotnet workload install maui` — run the explicit SDK manager commands only if the auto-setup misses something.
- Use `--list` flags liberally (`sdkmanager --list`, `dotnet workload search`) to discover current versions rather than hardcoding them.
- The `net9.0-*` TFMs assume .NET 9; substitute `net8.0-*` if using .NET 8.
